skill.name: allra-test-writing
skill.description: Guides Java Spring backend test writing for Allra standards, including helper selection, Fixture Monkey data setup, Given-When-Then structure, AssertJ, and Mockito.
skill.summary: Write consistent Java Spring backend tests using Allra test helper standards.
skill.author: Allra-Fintech

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and blocker patterns, but review found they are markdown backticks, Java examples, and test documentation. No prompt injection, credential access, network exfiltration, or malicious behavior was found. The skill includes benign Gradle and Maven test command examples, so external command risk is retained as a low-risk publication warning.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick alerts point to markdown inline code, markdown code fences, and Java test examples. They do not execute commands or define executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation formatting and Java example blocks, not executable skill scripts. The skill contains only SKILL.md and no runnable helper script.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is a Metadata False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert points to the frontmatter description. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing function, or security implementation is present at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive metadata for the skill. There is no code or cryptographic operation to evaluate there.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Java Test Method Names
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts match words inside Java test examples, such as test method names and repository calls. No commands collect host, user, network, or environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Java test method declarations or local test examples. No evidence of reconnaissance intent or data collection was found.

low_findings.title: Benign Local Test Command Examples
low_findings.description: The skill documents Gradle and Maven test commands for local project validation. These are normal developer workflows, but running project wrappers can execute local project code, so users should use trusted repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and limited to test execution. They are legitimate for a testing skill, but external command execution has inherent local-code execution risk.

content.value_statement: Backend teams need consistent Spring test choices and readable Java test structure. This skill guides helper selection, Fixture Monkey setup, Given-When-Then layout, AssertJ assertions, and Mockito patterns.
actual_capabilities: Selects Allra test helper types for integration, repository, controller, Redis, service, and domain tests.
Explains when to choose MockingUnitTest instead of IntegrationTest for Spring services.
Structures Java tests with Given-When-Then sections and descriptive display names.
Guides Fixture Monkey usage for generated test data and field overrides.
Shows AssertJ and Mockito patterns for assertions, mocks, exceptions, and verification.
Provides Gradle and Maven examples for running all tests, tagged tests, and specific test classes.
limitations: The guidance assumes Java 17, Spring Boot 3.2, JUnit 5, AssertJ, and Mockito.
Allra helper classes may not exist in every project and may need local equivalents.
The skill does not inspect runtime coverage or execute tests by itself.
Examples must be adapted to the application domain, package names, and repository APIs.

use_cases.title: Create Service Unit Tests
use_cases.description: Write fast service tests with Mockito, AssertJ, and clear Given-When-Then sections.

use_cases.title: Standardize Test Review
use_cases.description: Review whether new tests use the right helper type and avoid excessive integration tests.

use_cases.title: Improve Repository Coverage
use_cases.description: Plan repository and QueryDSL tests that verify database behavior and important constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Test Type
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to test this Java Spring feature: [describe feature]. Choose the Allra test helper and explain why.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Unit Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a JUnit 5 service unit test for [class and method]. Use Mockito, AssertJ, and Given-When-Then comments.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Integration to Mock Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this integration test and decide if it can become a MockingUnitTest. Keep only necessary integration coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Test Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a test strategy for this Spring workflow: [workflow]. Separate controller, service, repository, and integration tests.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to test simple discount calculation logic.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a MockingUnitTest because the logic can be isolated with mocked repositories. It suggests Given-When-Then sections, AssertJ value assertions, and Mockito verification.

output_examples.input: A reviewer asks whether a payment workflow needs integration coverage.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends IntegrationTest because money movement, rollback behavior, and multiple table consistency must be verified together.

output_examples.input: A team wants consistent generated test data.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests Fixture Monkey for object creation, with explicit field overrides for values that matter to the assertion.

best_practices: Start with MockingUnitTest for service logic unless transaction, money, or multi-table behavior requires integration coverage.
Use Given-When-Then sections and display names that explain the business scenario.
Mock external systems in integration tests and verify internal database state only when needed.
anti_patterns: Using IntegrationTest for simple validation, calculation, or single-repository logic.
Creating random test data without overriding fields that drive the expected result.
Writing assertions that only check non-null results without verifying business outcomes.

faq.question: Does this skill write tests for any language?
faq.answer: No. It is focused on Java Spring backend tests using Allra conventions.

faq.question: When should I use IntegrationTest?
faq.answer: Use it for money flows, transaction rollback, database constraints, multi-table consistency, event integration, or complex state changes.

faq.question: Can I use this without Allra helper classes?
faq.answer: Yes. Use Spring Boot test annotations directly and keep the same test structure and decision rules.

faq.question: Does it require Fixture Monkey?
faq.answer: No. Fixture Monkey is optional, but the skill explains how to use it when available.

faq.question: Does the skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents Gradle and Maven test commands, but execution depends on the user and tool environment.

faq.question: Is this safe for public marketplace use?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation-only examples and benign local test commands, with no malicious behavior.
